does: This paper identifies and analyzes a fundamental problem in sparse autoencoders (SAEs) used for interpreting language models: their single-direction decoder assumption causes multi-dimensional features to be artificially split across many latent codes. The authors provide two proofs of this phenomenon—a geometric argument showing exponential blowup in the number of atoms needed to reconstruct features with intrinsic dimension d≥2 to error ε, and an optimization analysis proving that the ℓ1-regularized SAE objective actively incentivizes descent toward this fragmented regime. They demonstrate empirically that this feature splitting occurs in practice, obscuring the true structure of learned representations and producing spurious interpretations.
connects: This work extends the emerging line of research applying SAEs to mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, building on prior work showing that SAEs can extract interpretable latent features from model activations. The paper directly responds to limitations in standard SAE formulations, which have become increasingly popular following recent work establishing their effectiveness for discovering monosemantic neurons and circuit components. It connects to broader research on representation geometry in deep learning and dictionary learning, drawing on classical results about atoms and bases in sparse coding. The optimization analysis builds on prior theoretical work on ℓ1-regularization and implicit bias in neural network training.
enables: The paper motivates the development of improved SAE architectures that respect the multi-dimensional structure of learned features, such as subspace-aware variants that assign multiple decoder directions per feature. This opens directions for more faithful mechanistic interpretability tools that can accurately recover feature geometry without artificial splitting. The theoretical framework provided—characterizing when and why feature fragmentation occurs—could enable better regularization schemes, stopping criteria, or architectural innovations for sparse coding in neural network analysis. More broadly, this work suggests that mechanistic interpretability methods must account for intrinsic feature dimensionality, potentially leading to more reliable circuit discovery and cleaner feature attribution in large language models.
